title: 2025/12/04 - TI (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-04 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - 2024/25 Annual Review of Maritime New Zealand [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual Review of Maritime New Zealand
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee 2024/25 Annual Review of Maritime New Zealand Hearing of evidence: Maritime New Zealand Kirstie Hewlett, Chief Executive & Director Dame Jo Brosnahan, DNZM, QSO, Chair Peter Brunt, Deputy Chief Executive, Regulatory Frameworks & Technical Support Graham MacLean, Deputy Chief Executive, Response Security & Safety Services 2024/25 Annual review of the Transport Accident Investigation Commission Hearing of evidence: Transport Accident Investigation Commission David Clarke, Chief Commissioner Martin Sawyers, Chief Executive Joelene Skelton, General Manager, Corporate Services 2024/25 Annual review of the Civil Aviation Authority of New Zealand Hearing of evidence: Civil Aviation Authority of New Zealand Mark Darrow, Board Chair Kane Patena, Director of Civil Aviation & Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual Review of Maritime New Zealand. Maritime New Zealand. Kirstie Hewlett, Chief Executive & Director

chunks.text: ... you can sit here. Unless you want to sit?

Right. Do you want to shut the door there, Jonathan? Thank you. W- Welcome. It's lovely to have you here. Um, if you want to do some introductions, um, we've got you down for an hour, um, so we'll, we'll see how we go in terms that timeframe. If we fill it up, we'll fill it up. If we go slightly shorter, we'll go slightly shorter.

But anyway, we, we, we're very interested in the maritime sector, as you know, because of our Ports Maritime Inquiry. So, um, if we want to ... w- w- we want to start off, maybe give, uh, say, five minutes or so intro of any key issues you want to raise and then we'll go to questions. Okay. Thank you very much. Thank you for the opportunity to be here. Um, Mr. Chair, um, members of the committee, uh, we, uh, (clears throat) sorry, appreciate the opportunity to meet with you today. I'm meeting with, um, I'm here with Kirsty

Hewlett, who's our chief executive, um, with Peter ... And she's also, of course, the director under the legislation. Peter Brunt, who's our DCE, uh, regulatory frameworks and technical support, and Graeme McVeigh, who is our DCE response security and, uh, safety services. So we hope that between us all, we can answer any of your questions. Um, so as you're aware that as the, sort of, guardian of New Zealand's ports, oceans and waterways, Maritime New Zealand, um, does deliver outcomes that are safe, secure, clean and sustainable for all New

Zealanders. Um, the past year has been one of, um, progress through collaboration and partnership with the sector, working smarter and delivering results across a range of areas. Um, the maritime sector, I would make the point, is key to New Zealand's livelihood. 99% of our imports and exports by volume and 79% by value go across our ports.

Uh, in particular, in '24-'25, we advanced key priorities aligned with the government and ministerial expectations and delivered on regulatory frameworks as part of our strategy. This progress was made despite a levy shortfall, uh, which we can talk about later, through careful planning and use of reserves and targeted savings enabled by improvements to our operating model. Uh, digitization has been and continues to be a major focus. Uh, we've made good progress on making it easier for those we regulate to interact with us

digitally. Our regulated parties can now report incidents from any digital device through an easy-to-use digital form. This, combined with improvements to how we triage and respond to notifications, including after hours, has meant people receive a timely and appropriate response. We've al- also made good progress on digitizing seafarer applications alongside changes to improved processes, rules and policies, uh, always with input from the sector.

This work will be launched along with a simplified website early in 2026. Um, these initiatives reduce time and cost, not just for regulated parties, but provide internal efficiencies for Maritime New Zealand while delivering safety outcomes. Uh, and in fact, we've recently even returned some of those saf- uh, those savings to applicants, which, um, the chief executive can talk about. We continued progress on our journey to implement our new regulatory approach focused on preventing harm. This has involved targeting our regulatory activity to the

areas of greatest harm to people, the marine environment, property and economy. It's also meant deploying a broader range of corrective and enforcement tools, creating better transparency about how and why we carry out our role and importantly continuing to strengthen our partnerships with the sector through multi-year harm prevention programs of interventions.

Um, these harm prevention programs are delivering results. Uh, fertili- f- fatalities in the commercial sector have continued to decline since '21-'22. Uh, we extended our health and safety designation across all 13 commercial ports, taking up an increased, um, responsibility there and with the Port Health and Safety Leadership Group, we launched the approved code of practice for loading and unloading of cargo at ports and on ships to create ba- baseline safety standards in ports. And I would say that, as a board, we're very proud

of the approach that our exec take in terms of, um, a collaborative leadership approach with the sector in terms of getting these sub-types of things into place very quickly and, and with a, a, a s- an aligned sector. In the fishing sector, we established the, um, Fishers, uh, Health and Safety Leadership Group and a work program based on critical risks. Our first winter campaign targeting recreational boaters, Better Your Odds, highlighted heightened risks outside the summer season.

We also supported an efficient and sustainable maritime sector prioritizing, uh, progressing priority rule amendments identified in conjunction with the sector, including workforce initiatives to ensure that maritime sector needs are understood and catered for, and pathways for new and low-carbon technologies, including lithium battery safety. We continue to, uh, engage internationally to influence changes to the international conventions that New Zealand has signed, and over the coming year, we'll be focused on ensuring planned changes to those conventions

support and enable New Zealanders in our maritime industry to thrive. And Kirsty's recently-... returned from the IMO and can talk further about that. Close to home, we continue our support for Pacific Island countries and have been actively exploring ways- Mm.... to deliver maximum value for the region.

Our readiness and response capability was tested- Mm.... during the year with significant maritime events, including the Aratiri and the Manaho, uh, groundings and the HMNZS Manawanui sinking in Samoa. These responses demonstrated our ability to deploy quickly, and lessons learned are informing the development of our next version of our integrated incident readiness and response strategy.

So, I want to acknowledge on behalf of the board the real dedication of Kirsty and her team and our staff and the collaboration of our sector partners. I think together we are building a safer, more resilient, and sustainable, sustainable maritime system, uh, for Aotearoa. So thank you.

Thank you, Kirsty and I are very happy, um, to answer any questions. We have a few questions. I, I'm actually going to give myself the luxury of the first one. Oh Um, so just in terms of the IMO engagement, uh, there's parts of our, part of our, as part of our ports and maritime, um, uh, inquiry, one of the things that's come through very clearly is the timeliness of shipping getting to New Zealand is very poor. Uh, by the time it's got out of New Zealand, it's worse.

Um, but one of the things which we've been told quite regularly is the IMO standards around the speed that vessels are a- able to travel is a problem. In other words, there's a maximum speed. If you go beyond that, you start getting, um, penalized financially. Um, in terms of the engagement there, and I think New Zealand's probably, because it's further away than anywhere else, uh, in the world, um, that part particularly suffers from that. Just wonder what conversations have been had or what levers we can (clears throat) try and change that, those

settings. Um, I don't think... I think that what you might be referring to is the zero emissions framework- It's, it's all related-... and poten- ... to zero emissions, yes. Potential measures. So that, that, that framework is not in. It was not agreed by the IMO. Um, so, um, what you might be getting is concerns for the future around, um, penalizing, um, over distance, uh, So that framework is not in place?

No, it's not. Um, I, I, um, I can't think of any other standards that you're talking about which would be penalizing that. That's the, that's the one that's being referred to, is definitely the carbon emission side of things. Yeah. So you're saying that's not in place? No, the IMO did not agree- Yeah.... um, to put in place the emissions framework.

That was deferred for a year- Right.... um, while more work would- Okay. ... could be done between the countries on that framework- Okay, well if-... and measures. Okay, so if it's deferred for a year, that means there's consideration still to come, so in your engagement with the, um, with that further consideration, what's the position that New Zealand is representing on that?

So we do not lead what the government's view is on the zero- Oh, that's right, yes. ... emissions framework and measures. We provide techn- technical advice and to the ministry- Yes.... who then feeds that into Cabinet, who makes decisions on how they want to- So, so that's-... approach them. ... that's something we should reflect, should ask the government rather than- Yes.... itself. Okay, that's What we do is technical standards that relate to things like methanol shipping- So, so-... response as well.

So just to finish my line off, why then would we be getting advice from various agencies that we've, uh, and companies that we've talked to, which are saying that that issue is a problem already, as opposed to it is one that we're fearing? You know, they just misunderstand- I'm not sure it would be IMO standards.

I think as- It was, uh, specifically IMO, specifically zero carbon. Uh, mm, no, I don't think- I, I think, I think it may be anticipating the effect of- Yeah. (clears throat)... a carbon price that would be implemented through essentially that standard. I think it is, it's an anticipation of concern, um, the way it's been reflected to us. Um, so it's more a concern about the future than the current settings.

Okay, that's very helpful. Thank you. Yeah. Tom. Yeah, thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thank you for coming in. Your annual report talks about the significant investment in reading this in response capability. Uh, and Dame Jo, you've, you've touched on the fact that that has been called upon over the, the last 12 months. Um, if we look at the context at the same time, the government has decided not to proceed with procuring a, uh, vessel that would add to, uh, resilience within the Cook Strait. So my question is, what advice, if any, or what involvement

has Maritime New Zealand had in providing advice to the government in reaching that decision not to procure, uh, emergency support, uh, which potentially might be at people's sort of times of, of need? I'll turn to her directly. So, yeah. So, um, we as, as you're probably aware, we did the detailed business case on potential options for emergency towage, and we also provided advice on the current arrangement that's in play. Um, the Ministry led the advice to ministers on the f- on the, um, decisions around, um, the, the ETV.

What, what does Maritime New Zealand identify as the risks associated with the government's decision? Um, so I guess it's up to the government, um, to make the choice on a cost-benefit anal- analysis around emergency towage. As the safety regulator, of course we would prefer to have an emergency towage vessel around the coast.

I understand that, but my question is like, what, what does Maritime New... I appreciate the government have made- Yeah.... this decision. Yeah. Or ministers have. Yeah. But from Maritime New Zealand's perspective, when you say that your preference would be to have that, uh, redundancy or resilience within the system, why would that be the case? Um, I think, uh, a- as you well know, there, there have been times, um, across the New Zealand coast over the last two to three years where an emergency towage vessel has been needed. One would be the Shilene, another would be Lara

Tedes, where the engine propulsion on both those vessels went, and it required an emergency towage vessel to tow it to port. Okay.So, in terms of mitigating that risk, what, what would you see as some of the options that would now be- (clears throat)... effectively at play if, if this resilience factor is being effectively, uh, canceled by the government?

So, um, as the Maritime Regulator, we will choose whatever assets are available around our coast in any situation. I, I can't foretell what those assets will be- Sure. ... at any one time. Um, there will, there is still an asset around the coast, um- Neither is I. ... but we will... W- W- There is still an asset-... around the coast at the moment, but, um, uh, we, we will use whatever we can, um, in eith- uh, ever which way we can in any kind of maritime incident readiness and response from what's available.

Okay, just one final question on that, Mr. Chair. In terms of there being an asset around the coast- Yeah.... what would be the maximum period of time, uh, for that vessel to get, depending on where it is of course- Yeah.... but you know how this all works. Mm-hmm. What would be the maximum period of time that it would take to get that vessel located from its sort of furtherest, uh, point to a situation in the Cook Strait?

So, uh, again, I don't know where the vessel will be. We'll call on whatever vessels are available, including the ones closest to the Cook Strait if an i- issue happened in the Cook Strait. Okay. But- Yeah.... is it accepted that without a dedicated resource located nearby or in the Cook Strait- (clears throat)... that this potentially could, um...

Well, that mitigation is something that the government would need to turn its mind to? That's a good question. I think it would... Again, it would depend on what vessel you're talking about, where the vessel was, what, um... And what assets there were, where they were on the coast. It's quite a difficult question, Sure. No, I appreciate that. Yeah. Can I just follow up on, on that one?

Which is, at the mo- so the vessel which we talk about is the, the one that's up at the, in Taranaki, um- Mm-hmm.... that's, uh, associated with the oil and gas fields? Yeah. If the oil and gas fields no longer exists or this, it's no longer needed by them, one assumes that it doesn't exi- that that vessel will then exit the country unless there's a reason for it to stay.

Yeah, that vessel may- Yeah, okay. So-... may, may leave, but there might be other reasons for- Okay, so- ... for vessels like that around our coast in the future. Okay, well, i- so, so I think the question- Wind farms, et cetera.... Tangi was, what Tangi was looking for, and I, I'm, I'm looking for the same, is, um, you would employ whatever vessels are around. Now, that sounds fine, but if the vessels... It's like saying, "Well, I'll go, go to my toolbox and get the saw out that isn't there." Um, you know, it's, it's no good using a hammer

when you needed a saw. Do we actually have vessels that will actually do the job if we need, uh... If the situation where, you know, you said, you mentioned the Shy Lane, there's the possibility of the rail ferries... And we, we know that they, they've been worked on, and there's new ferries coming, et cetera. Uh, I can't remember what you said the other, the other ship was. But if something goes wrong in that situation, are there actually vessels in the New Zealand network that actually could, perform a rescue operation? I'm just-

Uh, if, if you took the case of the Shy Lane, um, there, we were lucky there was a vessel around at that particular point in time. Actually it wasn't the MMA Vision. It was a different vessel. At the moment there is one vessel. If there was not a vessel around the coast of the type of the MMA Vision at the moment, um, that vessel would have had to have... We would have had to get something, or it would have had to get something from offshore to come and tow it.

So then we would rely on that, the vessel which was- Yeah. ... uh, in difficulty being... It would have to, um, m- (laughs) basically moor itself wherever it was, or, uh, somewhere safely as long as it could. Yeah. Does that constitute a, a real risk? And I'm, I'm thinking again, um, in the situation we had with, uh, rail ferries, um... It was Kai Taki wasn't it, which was off the shore of the Wellington, off the Wellington South Coast, which obviously lost propulsion for a while. The, the local tugs went out to, uh, uh, at least try and provide some stability as I understand it.

I don't know all the details. You will know those much more than I do. Mm-hmm. But, um, you know, do we, do we potentially put ourselves in a position where there is nothing available? I mean, getting a ship, getting a vessel from Australia- Yeah.... say, offshore- Yeah.... is not gonna get there very quickly. No. So- So yes, there would be a risk that there isn't a vessel that can come quickly.

And that the vessel which is in trouble is not able to secure itself in place, and is potentially at risk of hitting the, of, of running aground, or- Y- Y- E- Yes, it would depend how far close it was to the coast. Yeah. How, how significant is that risk in your view? Um, well, (laughs) I, I... That's a difficult thing to- Well, we-... to quantify, you know? (laughs) Well, no. I, I under- Yeah.

I understand that, but I mean, we, we make judgments about Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.... in, in all kinds of areas. I mean, is this something that's a very unlikely risk? Is it a high... You know, a, I mean, you could... If you did a risk matrix- Yeah.... what does it look like? I think it... I would, I would say it's a low risk- Okay.... but it's not no risk. Okay. It's a low risk o- It's a low risk of likely it could of happening, and if it does happen in that low risk situation, what is the likely consequence of that risk? You know, looking at the, the sort of matrix that

you usually do in terms of risk management? Yeah. A- again, it would depend where the vessel is. Hm. So even in, in the, in the event of the Kai Taki, we don't know for sure even if we had an ETV whether it would have got there in time. Sure. So you don't know. It would depend- Yeah. It will depend very much whether it's on open sea, on the coast, how close it is to the coast, whereabouts it is around New Zealand, all of those different kinds of factors.

Okay. Simon, and then Dan. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I'd like to change tack. Uh, I'd like to, uh, ask some questions about the regulatory safety and technical capability that Maritime New Zealand has. Uh, a number of stakeholders have raised with me concern that Maritime New Zealand does not have sufficient technical capability to undertake its role.

And I think it's important that we, we use this opportunity to actually flesh this out. Mm-hmm. So I'm interested, say, in the, in your executive team, uh, maybe cast a little wider than top three layers of management, how many individuals would have-... commercial maritime qualifications and how many of those senior staff, and say the top three layers of management, would have experience in commercial operations at sea?

Yeah. So, um, I think, uh, around my leadership team, we have two people who have, um, from the New Zealand Navy who have operated large vessels. In fact, one of them is sitting right behind me. Um, we have... We are a regulator, so when we have, uh, tier two and tier three functions, it very much depends the expertise we put on them as the ones that we need to be able to deliver something.

So, for example, uh, uh, someone that has run a large commericial- commercial ship won't be leading our Regulatory Frameworks team. So, very much we have a suite of capabilities across our organization. Many people who sit in our Technical Advice and Support Services function or are naval architects, um, who...

And same with our Maritime Inspections team, maritime engineers, master mariners, a whole raft of people that do those roles. They are in the right places of our organization to give us the technical advice and support when we need it to be able to deliver all of our functions. Okay. Yeah. So you've mentioned two former naval personnel.

Mm-hmm. My question was specifically around commercial maritime qualifications. Yeah. How many individuals in your senior leadership team, and, um, and maybe if we think about, you know, levels two, three, have got commercial maritime qualifications? You might need to come back to the committee with that answer. Yeah. Um, and secondly, how many of those people have had commercial maritime experience at sea as opposed to navy? (clears throat) Yeah. So, I... We can answer it for the leadership team, as I said, because there

are two from navy, and then at, uh, the rest of our leadership team, we can come back and give you the facts on that. So, so- But again, I stand behind recruitment of the right people to the right types of functions to be able to deliver our roles, our different roles, and I feel confident that we have the technical expertise and experience in the right parts of our organization for making those judgments and providing advice and support directly to me, the leadership team, and the board. Including the fact we have a chief technical officer with

commercial maritime experience across a range of all commercial vessels who will be able to, whenever we need expertise and advice, be able to provide that. In fact, has a d- dotted line directly to me. Further supplementary, Mr. Chair. Yep. Um, I'm looking at your recent engagement survey called The Voice of Us All, run from 14 to 28 March 2025. 92% of your staff participated, only 44% said the right people were rewarded and recognized at Maritime New Zealand.

Mm-hmm. How do you explain that? Um, I think, uh, what I would say with that question is that is an area where, um, we have done, uh, an action plan off the back of it. Mm-hmm. I think, um, a lot of people reflected in the comments on the survey that that was a very hard question to ask, because, uh, answer, because, um, people said they didn't know who the right people were. (laughs) Um, so that was quite a lot of the feedback on that.

But for me, what is really important is that people have career development opportunities in this organization- Mm-hmm.... and we invest quite significant money, um, in, um, being able to provide people with those development opportunities, either technically for their role or around leadership or support, um. Do you think that potentially the right people could have been people with commercial maritime experience or qualifications who are integral to the performance of Maritime New Zealand's operations?

So, uh, I'd- I'm not... I think that, uh, that question is relevant to all people across our organization. But again, I think we have, uh, a number of people who have got good commercial maritime, uh, experience and skills- (clears throat)... who are in senior roles in our lo- organization.

In fact, we've created some more roles in, in our Maritime Inspections team around- Mm-hmm.... management recently who are master mariners and master engineers as well. Okay. Um, Dan. Uh, thank you for your, um, uh, you and your organization for all you do for our maritime sector. Um, I just want to turn now to deficit. So, um, you've been running a deficit, uh, last year or the year under review.

Can I just clarify how many years Maritime New Zealand has been running financial deficits? Yeah. So, um, I think that's- the- that's a tricky question to answer because there's two different rationales for those. So, over the COVID period, you'll appreciate when there was no cruise ships coming, um, and yet, um, co- that cargo was variable. Um, the government did support us over that period, um, to make up the delta, um, while we were dealing with COVID. Um, when we were coming out of COVID, we had a maritime levy review. Um, it was...

Uh, we, we model, um, our levy, uh, revenue almost about 18 to 2 years out from when the levy comes in, um, and there was a lot of uncertainty around recovery of both crews and the economy coming out of COVID. Um, so when we established what we thought we would get for the levy, um, we probably, I think were, uh, um, uh, it turns out, overly optimistic on both financial recovery, but we've also seen, um, a 36 reduc- percent reduction in cruise visits in New Zealand.

Mm. Yeah. And that is what has led to that levy shortfall- (clears throat)... in our maritime levy. Um, how we've managed that maritime levy shortfall over the current levy period is through savings of 16% in our maritime levy, um, and through use of reserves to get us through this levy period. And then how we're dealing with, I guess, the sustainability or uncertainty through the next levy period is we have a levy review out for consultation at the moment.

Yes. Uh, so could I just clarify? So I, I, I take your response, um, seriously, but, um-From a, from a financial statement perspective, you're saying that since COVID, Maritime New Zealand has been in deficit? Is that what I understood? Yes, from- Okay.... this levy period which started 1 July 2024.

Okay. Yeah. And, what is the pathway back to surplus? Um, I think I understand this financial year you're forecast to be in deficit by about a million dollars? Um, is that- Could- Firstly, is that correct? And secondly, uh, what's the- Yeah.

... what's the forecast back then? Perhaps you could answer and then I should answer from a board point of view? Yes, that'd be great. 'Cause we obviously overview that. Yeah. Yeah. It's actually more like a $2.5 billion deficit. Um- Forecasts for this current- ... that has been used from the reserves. Yeah. The rest of the money, or the deficit or shortfall we had, we've made up from savings, so. Okay, so that's for this coming year?

Yeah. Is there, um, a medium turn pathway for the organization to balance the books? Um, you know, understanding the pressures- Yeah.... on the money you're not getting in from cruise ships and so forth? Is there a medium term pathway for the organization?

Yes, that's what the current levy review that's out for consultation is. It's got three options. Do nothing, which would mean we would have to reduce our maritime levy activity by another 20%. Uh, just get inflation, um, for the next three-year levy period, or deal with the shortfall and, um, uh, the inflationary cost pressures with a refund mechanism built in, so if the, if cruise or the economy recovers, actually that, um, shortfall will be decreased anyway.

Understood, and so you certainly take the fact that, um, you, there's revenue you're not getting in because of the economic conditions. Is, is the view of the organization, or one option to right-size the organization based on that shortfall? Could, could I just answer that? Yeah. We, we've already done that. We have done that, um, in fact, um, Kirsty and her team have, team have taken out about 5.6 million in savings to- Yes.

... in fact be able to help to meet that. One of the issues I think as a board we have to o- overview is that we, uh, obviously based on the funding review process, um, and it's only set every three years, when we, there, there will be some use of reserves to smooth that over that time, so we do expect that some years will be under and some will be over.

And so, we keep a very close eye on that. Yeah. And have conversations, if Kirsty will always tell us where she's finding the savings, and in terms of even the funding review that's out where she's already pr- been able to spell out what the consequences are, because when you're funded in the way we are, then if you know the money's not gonna come in, we've actually got to reduce our service.

And, um, and so we ha- we have to very carefully go through and determine where, where we can do that. So yes, the, Kirsty's done that exercise and the Board's considered that, so- Yeah.... that's part of the whole process. That's encouraging. And so, final question is, um, you know, around your savings, uh, exercise, is w- where are you, where is that coming from? Is it, um- Yeah.

... is it, is it staff numbers or is it programs and initiatives? Is it, um, you know, other? So maybe I can start by saying- (coughs) ... actually, we started our savings program before the last levy review. Yes. So, um, we actually have done an organizational strategy we call Te Korowai Kaitiakitanga which was actually looking at where we could be more efficient.

Right. So, the ty- types of things that we've done is we have reduced our property footprint in Wellington by 19%. Um, we've reduced our contractors and consultants this year. I think it's off total operating, it's at 5%, so very low. Um, we have, um, through reorganization and refocusing, got our overheads down to 26%, which is, uh, by both public and private sector considered efficient. Um, we have prioritized our travel, our learning and development, we've been digitizing our services, and yes, where, um, that has led to us removing

roles, we have done that as well. So, we have, I guess we've looked at all elements of our operating model to be able to get the savings out. Good. Hey, thank you very much. Yeah. Cheers. Tracey? Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, we see in the report that there's been a, um, an increase in recreational, um, deaths.

And I appreciate the fact that those numbers always, um, potentially going to ebb and flow a little bit. Mm-hmm. But from your perspective, um, have you had a look at what those kind of key risks are and the, the specific sort of causes? And are there any initiatives that you've been working on- Yes.... or that you've put in place to address, um, any of those, um, key risks or causes? Or, just tell us a little bit more about the work that's going on in that space?

Yeah, sure. And, um, the numbers have stayed relatively static over time, um, um, but we have seen increases in activity, so we think we're still, um, a death, one death is still too many, but we're actually seeing some impact. Um, so the things that we're particularly focused on at the moment is that we know that quite a lot of the deaths are happening close to shore.

So, we've got a big focus at the moment on initiatives around, um, ensuring that people are aware of that. We're worried about bar crossings. That's where a lot of the fatalities have happened. Um, we've also, as you'll, you'll be aware, um, we've been very keen on the wearing of life jackets. We do know that over the last, um, 10 years of, um, people have been wearing life jackets, the deaths would have been reduced by about 51%. Um- Actually, So, we, we run a whole raft of different things.

We've run behavior change campaigns- (laughs)... um, nationally. We, we also support those locally. Um, we, uh, work a lot with MetService to get messages out around safety and weather. Um, we run a grants program where, where based on our data and evidence, particularly around communities and risk areas, um, we provide funding for, um, people to undertake initiatives with those communities and report back on how they're delivering against our strategy. Um, and we work really closely with, um, harbor masters and regional councils on enforcement activity over

summer and education, um, campaigns on the water. Yeah. And I think it's, I think the life jacket issue is just really-... interesting because sometimes it doesn't feel all that rational that people make decisions not to wear a life jacket. Have, have you uncovered any kind of specific factors or, or trends or patterns even, uh, according to geography, according to, to any other demographics that would be helpful?

Yeah, I think, um, uh, demographic learning around the life jacket wearing, um, is that it is generally men over 40 years old, um, that are more likely to not wear a life jacket and to have fatalities. Could I just comment too that, of course there's a, a different approach depending on which harbor ma- you know, the different regional councils have different approaches in terms of absolute requirements.

So Auckland, you're required to carry them, but in Northland, you're actually required to wear them for s- for, for smaller vessels. So you know, you do have that difference. So is there anything sort of outside your control that could happen elsewhere by other people that would help you in your endeavor to reduce those numbers?

So I think, I think, uh, um, again, as you'll be aware, there's, there's conversations around life jacket wearing, around boating standards, around skipper licensing. Um, my name's David Clark. I'm the chair and chief commissioner of the Transport Accident Investigation Commission, or TAKE, as it's generally referred to. On my left is our chief executive, Martin Sawyer, Sawyers. And on my right is our general manager of corporate services, Joeline Skelton. So TAKE has, in effect, two roles. Uh, first and foremost, it's an independent Crown entity established under its own act

with the powers and obligations set out in the Crown Entities Act. But secondly, it's also a standing independent commission of inquiry, again, established under its own act, but with the powers of the Commissions of Inquiry Act. So we have those two roles in effect. Uh, Martin and his team, uh, operate as the secretariat for the Commission of Inquiry. And our primary role, in fact our sole role, is focusing on the safety of transport within New Zealand.

Um, we look, as was identified in, during the Maritime New Zealand presentation recently, we look at the systemic issues, safety issues that impact the New Zealand transport sector. And the, our purpose is to learn and communicate those learnings to the transport sector and others in order to primarily avoid repeat accidents. And our, our, um, purpose, our, um, is... and our strategy is focused on no repeat accidents ever.

Uh, that's a, uh, that's a vision that we aspire to, uh, that, as with all good visions, we aren't there yet, but we continue to work towards it. Um-We are independent and impartial, and our investigations have some specific, uh, characteristics that differ from the investigations that might be undertaken by, uh, other re- well, regulators within the sector such as the CAA, NZTA, police, and Maritime New Zealand.

First of all, we have no powers of compliance or, um, regulatory function. W- our recommendations are just that, recommendations. We require others to give effect to those, and so our focus in order to achieve that is credibility, accessibility, and readiness, so that when we make recommendations, they are listened to and acted upon. And that is how we impact the- the transport system.

Um, we exist also to meet New Zealand's international obligations under conventions in relation to maritime and, uh, civil aviation, which both require independent investigative bodies to investigate accidents and incidents. And importantly, it's not just accidents we investigate, but also incidents which haven't resulted in an accident but could.

And we apply certain criteria to the notifications we receive to determine whether or not we will open an investigation. The steps we take in an investigation, again, differ from a re- the regulatory function. We are not looking to blame. W- traditionally, investigative processes, uh, looked until they found an individual who made a mistake, and that was the end of the inves- uh, the investigation.

That was the cause. Much more modern techniques recognize that humans are fallible. Humans will make mistakes, and if the systems within which they operate can't accommodate those mistakes, there's a problem with the system, not the individuals. Now, on occasion, the problem does, yes, begin and end with an individual, but what we find is much more commonly it's the system that, uh, needs, um, uh, improvement or changes in order to accommodate the human factor within it.

We've had some major organizational programs over the last couple of years. Uh, one that we're o- we are continuing to work through at the moment is our major accident planning, which we are at the moment coordinating and adapting in order to, uh, align with the, uh, Coordinated Incident Management System which is in place in New Zealand so that we coordinate well with other organizations who will be involved in, uh, accidents and incidents in... or major accidents in New Zealand.

We have a unique position in that when an accident happens, TAKE, through its legislation, has primacy over the site and the evidence that's collected there. So, we also have arrangements with all the other organizations, police, Maritime New Zealand, CAA, NZTA. We have MOUs in place with them to coordinate our roles in the event of an accident and also to provide assistance to each other while at the same time maintaining our independence.

Uh, we've also embedded a new case management system that we received funding for in about 2021, '22 that has enabled us to become more efficient and effective in the way in which we produce reports and coordinate our evidence. It's allowed us to move a considerable distance in relation to the Digital First strategy, along with our recently released update to our website, which is now much more accessible to operators and the public to find our reports, to assess them, to understand what we're, what we're saying and the

recommendations we're making. It's important for us also to, as a, an organization, to recognize that our impact is not just in the recommendations and the reports we produce. Sometimes the most effective safety impact we have is commencing an investigation and communicating with the people involved during that investigation.

So, we don't wait 'til the publication of our report to let people in the sector and those involved in the accident know what the safety issues were. We're communicating with them throughout the investigative process. And perhaps another mechanism of looking at our effectiveness is to identify in our reports the number of safety issues identified that do not result in recommendations because the safety actions taken during the course of the investigation have resolved those issues.

And that's important because sometimes our reports can take some time to come out because of the complexity of the accident and the issues involved because, again, we look system-wide. We're not restricted to the particular accident or incident if it reflects a broader issue that might be common with other incidents and accidents.
